steps.title: Test the head-shape hypothesis
steps.content: The second team hypothesizes that intermediate-headed fish will be more likely than wide-headed fish to detect obstructions that create large vortices in low-visibility conditions. Because better detection would mean fewer collisions, a supporting finding should compare intermediate-headed and wide-headed fish under those exact conditions and show the wide-headed fish bumping into obstructions more often.

steps.title: Rule out weak comparisons
steps.content:
• Choice A - 'A study using obstructions that created large vortices in low-visibility conditions found that the Synodontis macropunctata, which has a relatively wide head, bumped into more than half of the obstructions.': This reports only the wide-headed fish's collision rate. Without an intermediate-headed comparison, it cannot support a claim about relative detection ability.
  • Choice B - 'A study using obstructions that created large vortices in low-visibility conditions found that some specimens of the intermediate-headed dusky smooth-hound (Mustelus canis) bumped into the obstructions more often than other specimens of the same fish did.': This compares fish within the same intermediate-headed species, not intermediate-headed fish against wide-headed fish.

steps.title: Use the direct evidence
steps.content:
• Choice D - 'A study using obstructions that created large vortices in low-visibility conditions found that the intermediate-headed dusky smooth-hound (Mustelus canis) bumped into the obstructions just as often as the wide-headed Synodontis macropunctata did.': Equal collision rates would undermine the predicted advantage for intermediate-headed fish.
  • Choice C - 'A study using obstructions that created large vortices in low-visibility conditions found that the wide-headed Synodontis macropunctata bumped into obstructions more often than the intermediate-headed dusky smooth-hound (Mustelus canis) did.': This directly compares the two head types and shows the wide-headed fish detected the obstructions less effectively. The answer is C.
